title: The London System
subtitle: One setup against everything: d4, Bf4, and the kingside attack

concepts.name: The London setup
concepts.short: d4, Bf4, e3, Bd3, Nf3, Nbd2, c3, O-O — the same harmonious build against almost any Black reply.

concepts.name: The good bishop on f4
concepts.short: The dark-squared bishop is developed OUTSIDE the pawn chain before ...e3 locks it in. Guard it; never trade it cheaply.

concepts.name: The c3-d4-e3 triangle
concepts.short: Pawns on c3, d4, e3 give a rock-solid centre: d4 is defended twice and the whole structure is hard to break.

concepts.name: Meeting ...c5 and ...Qb6
concepts.short: Black hits d4 and b2 with ...c5/...Qb6. Answer with c3 (propping d4) and Qb3 or Qc1 (covering b2) — don't panic.

concepts.name: The Ne5 outpost
concepts.short: The knight to e5 is the engine of the attack: it eyes f7, g6 and h7 and clears f3 for the queen.

concepts.name: The Greek Gift (Bxh7+)
concepts.short: Bishop takes h7, knight checks from g5, queen swings to h5. It only works when h7 has NO knight guard, g5 cannot be met by ...Bxg5 or ...Qxg5, and Black has no ...Nf6 to block — then it crashes through.

lessons.title: The Setup You Can Always Play
lessons.summary: Build the London piece by piece and learn WHY each move sits where it does.

intro.text: Before we build an opening, a reminder: the point of opening well is to reach a middlegame where tactics like THIS just fall into your lap. Win the loose piece.

segments.title: Walk the London, move by move

intro.text: I am Viktor. You will learn ONE system and play it against everything. Watch each move — I will tell you its job, not just its name.

coach.text: 1.d4. We claim the centre and, just as important, we OPEN the diagonal for our dark-squared bishop. That bishop is the soul of the London.
Black stakes a fair share of the centre. It does not matter much what Black plays — the London is a SYSTEM, the same setup against ...d5, ...Nf6, ...g6, all of it.
2.Bf4. The defining move. The bishop goes OUTSIDE the pawn chain — before we play e3. Get this wrong (e3 first) and the bishop is buried behind its own pawn for the whole game.
Black develops a knight. Normal, sensible.
3.e3. NOW the modest pawn move — the bishop is already out, so e3 only opens lines for our other pieces. It supports d4 and frees the f1-bishop.
Black mirrors. A typical, solid structure on both sides.
4.Nf3. Knight to its best square, controlling e5 — the square we want for OUR knight later. Always know which squares you are fighting for.
Black offers to trade the dark bishops. We will not oblige.
5.Bg3. We RETREAT rather than trade. The London bishop is our prized attacker — keeping it is worth a tempo. Note it still eyes the b8-h2 diagonal toward Black's king.
Black castles into safety. Note where that king now lives: g8, behind the h7-pawn. Remember that square.
6.Bd3. The light bishop takes aim at h7 — directly at Black's freshly-castled king. The two bishops now point like guns at the kingside.
Black completes development.
7.Nbd2. The last minor piece. It will reroute via f3 or f1 toward the kingside. Everything points one way — toward Black's king.

intro.text: Your turn. I will set the position; you play the natural London move.

coach.text: Black has just played ...d5. Develop the bishop to its home square — the one move that defines our whole opening.

successLine.text: Bf4. Outside the pawn chain, exactly right. ONLY now will we play e3.

retryLine.text: No. The bishop must come out BEFORE e3, or it is trapped behind its own pawns. Play Bf4.

coach.text: The bishop is safely out. Now play the modest pawn move that supports d4 and opens a path for your light bishop.

successLine.text: e3. The triangle c3-d4-e3 begins to form. Solid as a brick wall.

retryLine.text: Not yet. The move that frees the f1-bishop and props up d4 is the little pawn push e3.

coach.text: Wrong side to move? No — here YOU are Black for one move so you can see the trade offer. Black plays ...Bxg3, hitting your bishop. (Played for you next.) The real question is your reply.

successLine.text: Black trades on g3. Now: how should White recapture? That is the next position.

retryLine.text: Just play ...Bxg3 — we are studying how White answers the trade of the good bishop.

coach.text: Black took on g3. Recapture — but choose the pawn that OPENS a file toward Black's king, not the one that does nothing.

successLine.text: hxg3! Now the h-file is half-open and points straight at h7 and the Black king. The 'wrong' recapture fxg3 would have wasted that attacking gift.

retryLine.text: fxg3 just sits there. Take with the h-pawn — hxg3 — and the h-file becomes an attacking highway.

intro.text: Finish with tactics from real opening play. Develop, then strike.

lessons.title: When Black Hits Back: ...c5 and ...Qb6
lessons.summary: Black's most testing plan attacks d4 and b2 at once. Stay calm: c3 and Qb3 hold everything.

segments.title: The pressure on d4 and b2

coach.text: This is the critical structure. Black has ...c5 and ...Nc6 hammering d4; we have answered with c3, so d4 is defended by both the c- and e-pawns. The wall holds.
Now the real test: ...Qb6. The queen attacks d4 a THIRD time AND eyes b2, where our pawn is only loosely guarded. Beginners panic here. We do not.

intro.text: Two ways to meet ...Qb6 — I want you to find the principled one.

coach.text: Here Black has fianchettoed (...g6, ...Bg7) and still fired ...Qb6 at b2 — d4 is safe behind c3+e3, so the only real threat is ...Qxb2. Defend b2 AND offer to trade the attacking queen in one move. Where does your queen go?

successLine.text: Qb3 is the cleanest: it guards b2 and offers a queen trade — if Black takes, your b2-pawn recaptures and your rook gets the open b-file. (Qc1 also defends b2 and keeps queens on; both are fine.) The setup of Black's pieces changed, but the answer did not — that is the point.

retryLine.text: Defend b2 without losing the d4-pawn. The queen does both jobs: Qb3 (defends b2, offers a trade) — or the quieter Qc1.

coach.text: A different idea: Black has just captured on d4 with ...cxd4. Recapture — but with the pawn that keeps your structure and your bishop's diagonal healthy.

successLine.text: exd4! The e-pawn recaptures, the d4-pawn stands proud, and the f4-bishop keeps its lovely open diagonal. Recapturing with c3xd4 instead would block nothing but is less natural here.

retryLine.text: Recapture toward the centre and keep the bishop's diagonal open: exd4.

intro.text: When the enemy queen wanders out to grab a pawn, she often gets hit. Win material.

lessons.title: The Engine of the Attack: Ne5
lessons.summary: The London is not just solid — it attacks. The knight to e5 lights the fuse.

intro.text: A knight planted near the enemy king is worth a rook in attacking value. Find the blow.

segments.title: Why e5 is the dream square

coach.text: Both bishops aim at the kingside, the king is castled, and f3 is free. Time to leap. The knight to e5 is supported by nothing Black can easily challenge, and from e5 it touches f7, g6 and d7.

intro.text: Make the central leap that turns a solid position into an attacking one.

coach.text: A different London middlegame — Black chose ...c6 and slid a rook to e8 — but the picture is the same: bishops aimed at the kingside, king castled, f3 free. Make the same central leap on this new board.

successLine.text: Ne5! Same idea, fresh position. The knight dominates, clears f3 for the queen (Qf3-h3, or Qh5), and from e5 it eyes f7 and g6. If Black trades it off (...Nxe5 or ...Bxe5), dxe5 hits the f6-knight and grabs space — the outpost pays either way.

retryLine.text: The whole London points at the kingside. Send the f3-knight to the central outpost: Ne5.

intro.text: The attack is set — now land the tactic that ends it.

lessons.title: The Greek Gift: Bxh7+
lessons.summary: The sacrifice the London is built to deliver. Memorise the pattern; it wins games for life.

coach.text: Before you ever sacrifice, run the full checklist — six conditions, ALL required. THREE attackers: (1) bishop on d3 aimed at h7, (2) knight on f3 that can reach g5, (3) a clear path for the queen to h5. THREE defenders that must be ABSENT: (4) no Black knight guarding h7 — here our e5-pawn drove the f6-knight away, (5) no Black bishop that can answer Ng5 with ...Bxg5, (6) no ...Nf6 to block the mate. Tick all six and the gift is sound.

See WHY this Black position fails the defence test. The dark bishop is gone, so there is no ...Bxg5. The knight sits on a5, far away — it cannot jump to f6. And the e5-pawn covers f6, so even a knight could not block there. Every defender is missing. THIS is the difference between a winning sacrifice and a losing one — never play Bxh7+ until you have checked that Black cannot answer Ng5 with ...Bxg5, ...Qxg5, or ...Nf6.

segments.title: Deliver the gift, move by move

intro.text: Watch the machine run. Black's pieces are all on the queenside — the king has no help. Each move is forcing.

coach.text: 1.Bxh7+! The sacrifice. We give a bishop to rip open the king's cover. Declining with ...Kh8 just loses the pawn for nothing and leaves the attack — so the king must take.
1...Kxh7. The king is dragged out into the open air. Now the knight comes with tempo.

2.Ng5+! Check, and there is no ...Bxg5 or ...Qxg5 to answer it. The king must move. ...Kh8 allows Qh5 then Qh7#. ...Kg6 loses to Qg4 (the king is hunted down). ...Kh6 runs into Re3! with a decisive attack — Rh3 threats, and if ...Kxg5 then h4+/f4+ hunt the exposed king; White is winning. Best is ...Kg8 — back home.
2...Kg8. Back home — but the h7-shield is gone forever, and the knight on g5 is a monster one square from the king.

3.Qh5! The third attacker arrives, threatening Qh7#. There is no ...Nf6 to block it. Black's only parry is to give the king the f8-square with ...Re8 — and then the rook joins: Re3-f3-h3 swings to the open h-file and the attack is winning. The gift has cracked the king open.

intro.text: A FRESH position — Black's pieces sit differently (queen on c7, light bishop on d7, knight on c6) but every checklist condition still holds. Re-verify, then deliver all three moves of the sacrifice yourself.

coach.text: Run the checklist on the new geometry: bishop on d3 aims at h7, knight on f3 can reach g5, the queen is free. Defenders? Black's dark bishop is gone (no ...Bxg5), the e5-pawn covers f6 (no ...Nf6 block), and the c6-knight can't reach the kingside. All six conditions met. Start the sacrifice.

successLine.text: Bxh7+! The roof comes off — and you proved the gift was sound on a board you'd never seen.

retryLine.text: Begin with the sacrifice that removes the king's shelter: the bishop takes h7 with check.

coach.text: The king took on h7. Bring the knight in with check — and notice again Black has no piece that can answer with ...Bxg5 or ...Qxg5.

successLine.text: Ng5+! The king must run, and the knight sits one square from the queen's mating help.

retryLine.text: Check with the knight: Ng5+. It hits the king and aims at h7 and f7.

coach.text: The king fled to g8. Land the haymaker: bring the queen to the h-file with a mating threat on h7.

successLine.text: Qh5! Threatening Qh7#, and there is no ...Nf6 to block. Black must give the king air with ...Re8 (forced) — then White is completely winning. The Greek Gift, delivered on fresh ground.

retryLine.text: Bring the last attacker: Qh5, threatening mate on h7.

intro.text: Sacrifice to open the king. Calculate to the end before you commit.

lessons.title: Punishing the Bad Reply
lessons.summary: When Black plays carelessly, the London punishes immediately. Learn the two most common gifts.

coach.text: Black, impatient, has played ...e5 to challenge our bishop. But the pawn is loose. Watch how the bishop on f4 — out in the open, doing its job — collects it by force.

intro.text: Black has lashed out with ...e5. Refute it with a short forcing sequence.

coach.text: Black's ...e5 attacks the bishop but leaves the pawn under-defended. Start by taking in the centre.

successLine.text: dxe5! Now if ...dxe5 the queens come off — Qxd8+ Kxd8 — and then Bxe5 wins the pawn clean, because Black has lost the right to castle and we simply scoop e5.

retryLine.text: Take the centre pawn first: dxe5. The follow-up Qxd8+ and Bxe5 nets the pawn.

coach.text: The queens are off and Black's king has been chased to d8. Finish the job — grab the loose e5-pawn with the bishop that has aimed at it all along.

successLine.text: Bxe5! A clean extra pawn, the bishop dominant in the centre, and Black's king stuck on d8. THIS is why we put the bishop on f4 — it was always pointing here.

retryLine.text: Your f4-bishop has eyed e5 the whole game. Take the pawn: Bxe5.

intro.text: An undeveloped, uncastled enemy king is an invitation. Strike before he gets organised.

lessons.title: Typical Plans & The Whole Game
lessons.summary: Tie it together: which bishop to keep, which file to open, where to attack — then beat the engine with it.

segments.title: The three rules of every London

coach.text: Three rules. ONE: keep your dark bishop — retreat to g3, never trade it for free. TWO: when you can, recapture on g3 with the h-pawn to open the h-file. THREE: the plan is Ne5, then bring the queen to h5/h3 and the rook to the open file. Attack the castled king.

bossIntro.text: Now play it for real, with White. Build the setup — d4, Bf4 (before e3!), e3, Bd3, Nf3, Nbd2, c3, castle — keep your good bishop, then look for Ne5 and the kingside attack. Show me you understand the WHY, not just the moves.

questions.prompt: What is the single most important move-order rule of the London System?
options: Play e3 first, then develop the bishop behind it
Develop the bishop to f4 BEFORE playing e3, so it isn't trapped
Always trade the dark-squared bishop as early as possible
Castle queenside before developing any minor pieces
questions.explain: The dark-squared bishop must come out to f4 before e3 locks it behind the pawn chain. That bishop is the soul of the system.

questions.prompt: Black plays ...c5 and ...Qb6, hitting d4 and b2. What is the principled response?
options: Give up the b2-pawn and counterattack
Hold with c3 supporting d4, and Qb3 (or Qc1) covering b2
Trade everything on d4 to kill the attack
questions.explain: c3 props up d4 and Qb3 defends b2 while offering a queen trade. The wall holds; no need to panic or sacrifice material.

questions.prompt: What is the strategic point of the knight jump Ne5 in the London?
options: It offers an immediate trade to simplify into an endgame
It defends the king on g1
It plants a knight near Black's king and clears f3 for the queen to swing to h5/h3
questions.explain: Ne5 is the springboard of the attack: a dominant central knight that eyes f7/g6 and opens f3 for the queen to join the kingside assault.

questions.prompt: Which checklist tells you the Greek Gift (Bxh7+) is sound?
options: Black's queen is on the kingside defending
Bishop hits h7, knight can reach g5 and queen can reach h5; AND no knight guards h7, no bishop can play ...Bxg5, and there is no ...Nf6 block
You are behind in material and need a swindle
Black has castled and you have an extra pawn somewhere
questions.explain: All six conditions matter. The three attackers (Bd3, Ng5, Qh5) are useless if Black can defend: a knight guarding h7, a bishop that meets Ng5 with ...Bxg5 (or a queen that plays ...Qxg5), or a ...Nf6 block each refute the sacrifice. Only when every defender is missing does Bxh7+ crash through.

questions.prompt: 1.d4 d5 2.Nf3 Nf6. White to move — develop a piece the London way.
questions.explain: Bf4. The dark-squared bishop must come OUTSIDE the pawn chain before e3 is ever played — otherwise it is buried for the game. The knight already being on f3 changes nothing: Bf4 is still the move that gives the London its identity.

questions.prompt: White to move. The bishop is already outside the chain on f4. Continue the build.
questions.explain: e3. Only now — with the bishop safely out — does the modest pawn move come. It props up d4 and frees the f1-bishop; the c3-d4-e3 triangle takes shape.

questions.prompt: A more developed London — Black has ...Be7 and ...Nc6 out and has just lunged ...Qb6 at b2. White to move: find the principled reply.
questions.explain: Same antidote, new board: ...Qb6 really does threaten ...Qxb2 (b2 is only loosely held), while d4 is safe — c3 and e3 guard it, so ...Qxd4 is impossible. Qb3 covers b2 and offers the queen trade; Qc1 is the calm alternative that also defends b2 and sidesteps ...Na5. Either holds — derive it, don't panic.

questions.prompt: Black has just captured on d4 with ...cxd4. White to move — recapture.
questions.explain: exd4 keeps a proud pawn on d4 and, crucially, leaves the f4-bishop's diagonal wide open. Recapturing with the c-pawn would be passive and block nothing useful.

questions.prompt: A London middlegame against a fianchetto setup. White to move — find the strongest plan.
questions.explain: Ne5 — the same dream outpost even though this is a brand-new structure. The knight dominates the centre, clears f3 for the queen, and points at f7 and g6. Recognising this leap in an unfamiliar position is the whole point.

questions.prompt: White to move. Before you decide, run the Greek-Gift checklist yourself — then play the best move.
questions.explain: Bxh7+! is sound here: the bishop hits h7, the knight reaches g5, the queen can swing to h5 — and Black has NO knight on f6 (the e5-pawn covers f6), no dark bishop for ...Bxg5, and no ...Nf6 block. Every defender is missing, so the gift crashes through. You had to verify the checklist, not just recognise the shape.

questions.prompt: Tempted by the kingside, White played Bxh7+ here. Correct sacrifice, or a blunder?
questions.explain: Blunder. The checklist fails on condition four: Black's knight is still on f6, guarding h7. After ...Nxh7 White has simply given away a bishop for one pawn and has nothing. The Greek Gift only works when NO knight defends h7 — recognising when it does NOT apply is as important as knowing when it does.

questions.prompt: Black offered a trade with ...Qb6. White answered Qb3, defending b2 and offering to swap queens. Correct or a blunder?
questions.explain: Qb3 is exactly right: it guards b2, hits d5, and offers the trade of Black's most active piece. Nothing to fear in this structure.

questions.prompt: Black played ...Bd6, offering to trade your dark-squared bishop. White answered Bxd6, taking the trade. Correct or a blunder?
questions.explain: Blunder of judgment: the f4/g3 bishop is the London's prized attacker. You should retreat with Bg3 and keep it — trading it away surrenders your main attacking piece.

questions.prompt: Black just played ...Bxg3, trading on g3. White recaptured with hxg3. Correct or a blunder?
questions.explain: hxg3 is the thematic recapture: it half-opens the h-file straight at Black's king, exactly the file you want for the attack. fxg3 would waste that gift.
